chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: A Journey through States of Water

content.text: Ice and water are two forms of the same substance, called states. These different states show many differences in their behaviour, such as water's ability to flow and splash, which ice does not possess.

content.title: Observing States of Water
procedure: Put an ice cube in a cup.
Leave it on the table and observe.
content.conclusion: Ice and water are two forms (states) of the same substance. They behave differently despite being the same substance.

content.text: When water disappears from surfaces like puddles, wet utensils, or hot pans, it is not always absorbed. Often, it converts into a gaseous state.

content.title: Investigating Water Seepage
procedure: Take a tablespoon of water on a steel plate.
Observe if water seeps through to the other side.
Keep observing until the water completely disappears.
content.observation: Water does not seep through the steel plate but eventually disappears from the surface.
content.conclusion: Water converts into a gaseous state called water vapour.

content.title: Water Vapour and Steam
content.text: The process of conversion of water into its vapour state is called evaporation. Steam is water vapour; it becomes visible due to the presence of tiny droplets of water within it.

content.title: Examples of Evaporation
items: Hand sanitiser disappearing when rubbed on hands.

content.text: When water vapour in the air comes into contact with a cold surface, it turns back into liquid water.

content.title: Observing Condensation
procedure: Take cold water in a glass tumbler.
Add a few ice cubes into it.
Leave it undisturbed for five minutes and observe the outer surface.
content.observation: Tiny water droplets appear on the outer surface of the glass tumbler.
content.conclusion: Water vapour from the air condenses upon hitting the cold surface of the glass.

content.title: Measuring Mass in Condensation
procedure: Place a tumbler of ice-cold water on a digital weighing balance.
Record the weight every five minutes for 30 minutes.
content.observation: The mass shown on the balance increases over time.
content.conclusion: The increase in mass is due to water vapour from the air condensing onto the tumbler, adding 'extra' water from the atmosphere.

content.title: Humidity
content.text: The amount of water vapour present in the air is known as humidity. Humidity levels vary with the weather and location.

notes.section_title: Properties of the Three States of Water

content.text: Other substances like wax, oil, and ghee also exhibit these three states depending on the temperature.
States of water change based on whether heat is added or removed.

items: Melting: Conversion of solid (ice) to liquid (water) on heating.
Freezing: Conversion of liquid (water) to solid (ice) on cooling.
Evaporation: Conversion of liquid (water) to gas (vapour) on heating.
Condensation: Conversion of gas (vapour) to liquid (water) on cooling.

content.title: Effect of Exposed Area
procedure: Take equal amounts of water in a bottle cap and a plate.
Record the time taken for complete evaporation.
content.observation: Water in the plate evaporates much faster than in the bottle cap.
content.conclusion: Increasing the exposed surface area increases the rate of evaporation.

content.title: Conditions for Faster Evaporation
items: Increased air movement (e.g., using a fan or a windy day).
Lower humidity (dry air vs rainy/humid day).

content.text: Evaporation absorbs heat from the surroundings, creating a cooling effect. This is why we feel cool under a fan when we sweat.

content.title: The Earthen Pot (Matka)
content.description: Water seeps through the tiny pores of an earthen pot and evaporates from the outer surface. This process takes heat from the water inside, making it cold.

content.title: Pot-in-Pot Cooler
procedure: Place a smaller earthen pot inside a larger one.
Fill the gap with sand and keep the sand moist with water.
Cover the top with a lid or wet jute sack.
content.observation: Vegetables and fruits kept inside remain fresh and cool.
content.conclusion: The evaporation of water from the moist sand cools the inner pot without electricity.

content.text: Water vapour is lighter than air and rises. As it goes higher, the air becomes cooler, causing the vapour to condense into tiny droplets around dust particles.

content.title: Cloud Formation
content.text: These floating droplets form clouds. When droplets join to form larger, heavier drops, they fall as rain, hail, or snow.

content.title: Cloud in a Bottle
procedure: Squeeze and release a plastic bottle with water.
Repeat after adding a small burnt piece of newspaper (smoke/dust).
content.observation: Haziness (clouds) forms inside the bottle in the second case.
content.conclusion: Dust or smoke particles provide surfaces for water vapour to condense into clouds.

content.text: The continuous circulation of water from the Earth's surface (oceans, lakes, rivers) into the atmosphere and back to Earth as precipitation is known as the water cycle.

glossary.definition: The process of conversion of water into its vapour state.
The process of conversion of water vapour into its liquid state.
The process of conversion of a solid into liquid state.
The process of conversion of liquid into solid state.
The amount of water vapour in the air.
The circulation of water between the Earth's surface and the atmosphere.

summary_points: Water exists in three states: solid (ice), liquid (water), and gas (water vapour).
Heat causes evaporation and melting; cooling causes condensation and freezing.
Evaporation rate depends on surface area, temperature, wind, and humidity.
Evaporation produces a cooling effect, used in earthen pots and natural cooling.
Clouds form when water vapour condenses around dust particles in cooler higher altitudes.
The water cycle is the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the surface of the Earth.
